lesson.menu_title: Reading the Signs -- Guessing What Is Going On When You Are Abroad
lesson.menu_desc: Speculating about people and situations with modals of deduction -- 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Reading the Signs
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 13 -- Reading the Signs

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it is Solveig. So, the mystery at the harbor this morning. I asked around, and I was completely wrong at first. I thought it must have been a fishing boat in trouble, because of all the people. But no. Somebody spotted a pod of orcas in the fjord, right by the pier. They come in maybe once or twice a winter, chasing the herring, so it is a rare thing. That old man who had been standing there for hours? It turns out he must have known they were coming, because he is a retired whale researcher. Can you believe it. I jumped to conclusions and imagined the worst, and it was something wonderful all along. That is the lesson, I suppose. When you cannot ask, you guess, and half the time the guess is wrong. Anyway, they might still be there tomorrow. Come early. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, the traveler's sixth sense, and why guessing is a skill worth practising. Here is something that happens the moment you land in a country whose language you do not speak. You become a detective. A shop is shut in the middle of the afternoon. It must be a holiday. Everyone is carrying flowers in one direction. There must be a wedding, or a funeral. A stranger keeps glancing at you and smiling. Odds are you have something in common, or you remind them of someone. You read the signs, you form a hunch, and you act on it. And here is the interesting part. You are wrong a lot. The shut shop was just the owner taking a long lunch. But being wrong is not the point. The point is that guessing keeps you curious and keeps you engaged. So do not be afraid to take a wild guess. Read between the lines. Because a traveler who guesses is a traveler who is paying attention.
